weekly_overview.raw_line: What to watch during the week of Monday 4/27 - this week is extremely busy , with a slew of central bank decisions , a lot of critical economic data , and a ton of earnings . The key macro events include the BOJ decision (Mon night), the FOMC decision (Wed afternoon), China’s NBS PMIs for Apr (Wed night), the ECB and BOE decisions (Thurs morning), the US PCE/GDP for Q1 and PCE for Mar (Thurs morning), and the US manufacturing ISM for Apr (Fri morning). The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( VZ ); Mon night ( AVB, CCK, CDNS , and NUE ); Tues morning ( GLW, GM, HLT, KMB, KO, SHW, SPGI, SYY, UPS , and ZBH ); Tues night ( BKNG, BXP, HOOD, MDLZ, NCPI, OMC, SBUX, STX, TER, TMUS , and V ); Wed morning ( ABBV, ADP, APH, CTSH, EME, ETSY, GD, GEHC, HUM, PSX, SWK, WING , and YUM ); Wed night ( AMZN, CMG, EQIX, F, GOOGL, KLAC, MAT, META, MGM, MSFT , and QCOM ); Thurs morning ( APD, BAX, BMY, CAH, CARR, CAT, CI, COP, HUBB, LLY, MA, MO, MRK , and RCL ); Thurs night ( AAPL, AIG, AMGN, CLX, COHU, DXCM, GDDY, SNDK , and WDC ); Fri morning ( AON, ARES, CL, CVX, EL, LIN, LYB, and XOM ); and Sat morning ( BRK’b ). The key European reports include: Tues morning ( Airbus, Anglo American, Barclays, BP, Novartis , and WPP ); Wed morning ( Adidas, Astra Zeneca, Deutsche Bank, GSK, Mercedes, Norsk Hydro, Santander , and UBS ); Wed night ( Universal Music Group ); and Thurs morning ( Arcelor Mittal, BNP, Schneider Electric, Soc Gen, Unilever , and VW ).

The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( VZ ); Mon night ( AVB, CCK, CDNS , and NUE ); Tues morning ( GLW, GM, HLT, KMB, KO, SHW, SPGI, SYY, UPS , and ZBH ); Tues night ( BKNG, BXP, HOOD, MDLZ, NCPI, OMC, SBUX, STX, TER, TMUS , and V ); Wed morning ( ABBV, ADP, APH, CTSH, EME, ETSY, GD, GEHC, HUM, PSX, SWK, WING , and YUM ); Wed night ( AMZN, CMG, EQIX, F, GOOGL, KLAC, MAT, META, MGM, MSFT , and QCOM ); Thurs morning ( APD, BAX, BMY, CAH, CARR, CAT, CI, COP, HUBB, LLY, MA, MO, MRK , and RCL ); Thurs night ( AAPL, AIG, AMGN, CLX, COHU, DXCM, GDDY, SNDK , and WDC ); Fri morning ( AON, ARES, CL, CVX, EL, LIN, LYB, and XOM ); and Sat morning ( BRK’b ). The key European reports include: Tues morning ( Airbus, Anglo American, Barclays, BP, Novartis , and WPP ); Wed morning ( Adidas, Astra Zeneca, Deutsche Bank, GSK, Mercedes, Norsk Hydro, Santander , and UBS ); Wed night ( Universal Music Group ); and Thurs morning ( Arcelor Mittal, BNP, Schneider Electric, Soc Gen, Unilever , and VW ).

source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Wednesday April 22, 2026
market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures indices are rallying so far this morning: S&P +43 points/61bp, Dow +253 points/51bp, Nasdaq +213 points/80bp, and R2 K +25 points/91bp. The major Eurozone indices are about flat overall , with underperformance in staples, banks, retail, luxury, healthcare, and telecom while utilities, energy, real estate, tech, chemicals, and basic resources outperform . Akzo Nobel, ASM Int’l, Danone, K+S, and Randstad are all rallying on earnings. Reckitt Benckiser is getting hit on back of its report. Stocks in Asia saw mixed price action on Wednesday: TPX -0.67%, NKY +0.4%, Hang Seng -1.22%, HSCEI -1.59%, SHCOMP +0.52%, Shenzhen +1%, Taiwan TAIEX +0.73%, Korea KOSPI +0.46%, Australia ASX 200 -1.18%, and India is down 70-80bp. Treasuries are pretty flat across the curve, and Fed expectations are holding steady. The market is pricing in ~9bp of FOMC rate cuts this year. Brent is climbing 110bp despite the indefinite ceasefire extension. Gold is advancing 90bp while silver rises 200bp. Bitcoin is spiking 310bp (to ~$78 K).
•
whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – US equity futures have a modest bid after Trump indefinitely extended the ceasefire while media reports suggest the two sides have significantly narrowed their differences on a deal (although an Axios article said the suspension isn’t actually indefinite , and the White House is demanding engagement from Iran within the next few days). However, intense distrust between Washington and Tehran , along with the ongoing blockade , are preventing talks from taking place , and raising the risks of unintended escalation (Iran fired on two container ships overnight). Away from geopolitics, the big area of focus is on earnings . In aggregate, results for the Q1 season have been coming in strong , with companies performing well despite the Iran/Hormuz-linked geopolitical pressures . However, travel-linked firms are experiencing a massive cost burden given what’s happening w/oil ( United Air and TUI AG both cut their full-year outlooks because of oil). Some of the big upside standouts this morning on the earnings front include ABB, Akzo Nobel, ASM Int’l, ASMPT, CB, Danone, K+S, MANH , and Randstad . Some of the big downside standouts this morning on the earnings front include CALX, COF , HCL Tech , and Reckitt Benckiser .
•
watching_next:
What we’ll be watching on Wednesday – our focus will be on earnings before the US open ( BA, BSX, ELV, GEV, MCO, OTIS, PM, T, TEL , and VRT are the highlights), earnings after the European close ( Essilor Luxottica and L’Oreal are the highlights), the Google Cloud conf. opening keynote (12pm ET), and earnings after the US close ( IBM, LRCX, LUV, LVS, NOW, TSLA, TXN , and URI are the highlights).
• Market in a Minute (macro) - The US and Iran have actually made significant progress behind the scenes on key issues (including Iran’s nuclear program) and could strike a deal fairly quickly if/when the two sides agree to meet ( WSJ ); Trump’s ceasefire suspension is “not going to be open-ended” and Iran only has “another 3-5 days” to engage in negotiations (White House officials still believe a deal is possible but are encountering difficulties in negotiating given divisions within the Tehran gov’t ) ( Axios ); A White House official said there is still a chance that US and Iran hold face-to-face meetings soon , and a trip could be arranged quickly if Tehran gives indications it is prepared to negotiate ( CNN ); Iran’s military still maintains significant capabilities despite the recent US/Israeli bombing campaign ( NBC News ); US has significantly depleted its stockpiles of critical missiles during the war with Iran ( CNN ); Wall Street banks are accelerating job cuts thanks in part to AI adoption ( NYT ).
• Market in a Minute (micro – Wednesday morning) - ABB shares rally in Europe after the company reported solid upside in Q1 on sales (+11% vs. the Street +9.3%) and esp. bookings (+24% vs. the Street +9.3%), and boosted its full-year guidance (“Market momentum remains strongest in the data center segment, but also positive for grid investments”) ( ABB ); Akzo Nobel shares rally in Europe after the company reported solid upside in Q1 on both sales and earnings/margins as the company benefited from price increases and cost controls (“While the conflict in the Middle East is impacting our cost of supply, we’re maintaining our guidance for the year. Our already announced price increases are expected to fully compensate anticipated cost impacts based on current assumptions”) ( Akzo Nobel ); ASM Int’l shares spike in European trading after the company reported strong upside in Q1 on sales and margins (“AI-led demand accelerated further in the quarter, underpinned by strong customer investments in capacity to drive long-term AI infrastructure expansion”), and provided a Q2 sales outlook is ahead of expectations too (they see sales of EU980 MM vs. the Street EU887 MM); BHP now sees copper production at the upper-end of its prior guidance while the iron ore production forecast was reiterated, and the company signaled it was nearing a supply deal agreement w/China ( BHP ); Danone shares trade well in Europe after the company posted decent sales (+2.7% vs. the Street +2.65%) despite headwinds from the Middle East war and the baby formula recall, and reiterated its full-year guidance ( Danone ); HCL Tech shares tumbled in India after the company reported underwhelming results and provided disappointing guidance ( Reuters ); K+S issued an upside preannouncement on Q1 EBITDA and free cash flow and boosted its guidance for the year; Karex , the world’s largest condom maker, said it would be forced to hike prices by up to 30% due to input cost increases stemming from the Middle East war ( FT ); Randstad shares spike in Europe after the company reported upside on Q1 organic revenue growth (+0.4% vs. the Street -0.3%) (“While geopolitical instability and limited visibility require us to remain vigilant, the trajectory in Q1 with 0.4% growth gives us confidence for the months ahead”) ( Randstad ); Reckitt Benckiser shares tumble in European trading after the company reported a shortfall on Q1 sales (+0.6% vs. the Street +1.5%) due to a mild cold and flu season, ongoing challenges in Europe, and geopolitical disruptions (the company left its full-year guidance unchanged) ( Reckitt ); TUI AG cut its guidance for the FY due to fallout from the war in the Middle East (“the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the uncertainty surrounding its duration continue to limit near-term visibility and drive consumer caution”) ( TUI AG )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Tuesday night) - ADBE (Adobe) announced a $25 B buyback authorization, which is 25% of the market cap at the present time (“Our new $25 billion share repurchase authorization is a direct expression of confidence in our robust cash flow and the long-term value we are delivering to investors”); CALX (Calix) reported modest upside on Q1 EPS/revenue (40c/$280 MM vs. the Street 38c/$277.5 MM) and added $100 MM to the buyback, but the Q2 guidance is mixed, w/better revenue ($290 MM vs. the Street $281 MM) but slightly weaker EPS (40c vs. the Street 42c) as memory prices weigh on margins; CB (Chubb) reported decent upside on Q1 EPS (6.82 vs. the Street 6.60), w/the beat driven by a slightly favorable combined ratio, but the big standout of the report is the robust net premium growth performance; COF (Capital One) reported a shortfall on Q1 adjusted EPS at $4.42 (vs. the Street $4.56), with the miss driven by slightly weaker revenue (the NIM/NII came in below plan) and slightly higher provisions ($4.068 B vs. the Street $3.8 B), while they controlled expenses very well; ISRG (Intuitive Surgical) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS (2.50 vs. the Street 2.10) and sales ($2.77 B vs. the Street $2.61 B), and the full-year guide is tweaked up slightly; IPAR (Interparfums) reported Q2 revenue of $345 MM, a bit below the Street forecast of $354 MM, w/the Middle East war a ~1% headwind to the top line; MANH (Manhattan Associates) reported decent upside on Q1 EPS/revenue (1.24/$282 MM vs. the Street 1.12/$274 MM), and RPOs were better too, and the full-year guidance is increased; SON (Sonoco) reported EPS inline w/the Street at 1.20 (vs. the Street 1.19), but EBITDA fell short ($277 MM vs. the Street $287 MM), and mgmt. now sees EPS at the low-end of the prior $5.80-6.20 guidance range; TFX (Teleflex) – CVC and GTCR are exploring a bid for Teleflex ( Bloomberg ); UAL (United Air) reported modest upside on Q1 adjusted EPS (1.19 vs. the Street 1.09) and while the Q2 EPS guidance range falls short (they see $1-2 vs. the Street $1.75) due to the huge jump in fuel costs, the 2026 EPS mid-point of $9 is actually not far from the consensus forecast of $9.08, which is encouraging considering what’s happening with oil; WFRD (Weatherford) reported a large beat on EPS ($1.49 vs. the Street $1.03), but revenue/EBITDA weren’t that far ahead of plan ($1.15 B/$233 MM vs. the Street $1.145 B/$228.5 MM), and mgmt. warned that Q2 will be softer than anticipated because of Middle East disruptions; Space X enhances the appeal of x AI with the (potential) purchase of Cursor ( Vital Knowledge )
• Calendar for Wednesday 4/22 – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( BA, BKU, BSX, CME, DCOM, ELV, GEV, MAS, MCO, MHO, NVR, ONB, OTIS, PM, T, TEL, TMHC, TNL, VRT, WAB ), analyst meetings ( CALX ), earnings after the EU close ( Carrefour, Essilor Luxottica, L’Oreal ), the Google Cloud conference (Apr 22-24; opening keynote is 12pm ET on Wed), earnings after the US close ( AZZ, BANC, BANR, CACI, CCI, CHDN, CSX, FAF, FR, FULT, GGG, GL, GTY, HLX, HXL, IBM, KALU, KMI, KNX, LBRT, LRCX, LUV, LVS, MEDP, MOH, NOW, OII, PKG, RJF, ROL, RS, TSLA, TXN, URI, WEX ), and South Korea’s Q1 GDP report (Wed night).
Iran

• Trump issued a social media post at ~4:10pm ET in which he said the ceasefire deadline has been postponed indefinitely (it was expected to expire Wed night). After insisting in recent days that the deadline would not be moved, Trump stayed true to his word – he eliminated it instead (please note the sarcasm in the preceding sentence). As has been clear for weeks, Trump is stuck – he has no good options to kinetically escalate (which is why he continues not to do so), he can’t simply exit , and the status quo (with Hormuz shut) isn’t tenable , and as a result, his only realistic path forward is pushing for some type of a “deal” , and it’s likely one arrives in the relatively near term (we put “deal” in quotes as whatever gets announced won’t be much different than a grudging, nonbinding détente between the two sides with some deliverables but nothing that solves the core problems in perpetuity ). While there is still a bit of skepticism and cynicism in the market about Iran , most are of the view that Operation Epic Fury is past its peak , with an agreement of some sort more likely than not .
• Trump’s ceasefire suspension is “not going to be open-ended” and Iran only has “another 3-5 days” to engage in negotiations (White House officials still believe a deal is possible but are encountering difficulties in negotiating given divisions within the Tehran gov’t ) ( Axios )
• The US and Iran have actually made significant progress behind the scenes on key issues (including Iran’s nuclear program) and could strike a deal fairly quickly if/when the two sides agree to meet ( WSJ )
• A White House official said there is still a chance that US and Iran hold face-to-face meetings soon , and a trip could be arranged quickly if Tehran gives indications it is prepared to negotiate ( CNN )
• Iran’s military still maintains significant capabilities despite the recent US/Israeli bombing campaign ( NBC News )
• US has significantly depleted its stockpiles of critical missiles during the war with Iran ( CNN )
• Global oil market will lose ~1 B barrels of crude and refined products due to the war in Iran, even if the conflict ended tomorrow ( FT )
• Global food market at risk of a significant shock due to the ongoing closure of Hormuz ( FT )
• At least 34 tankers with links to Iran have managed to travel in/out of Hormuz since the US blockade commenced ( FT )
US macro/Washington

• We didn’t think Warsh’s testimony on Tuesday was particularly notable , with most of his answers either anodyne or vague (he wants rates lower and the balance sheet smaller but didn’t provide much specificity around either policy objective). The real issue w/Warsh is that he probably won’t be confirmed by the time Powell’s term ends on May 15 , which could prompt Trump to (attempt to) fire Powell as chair (if the DOJ investigation into the Fed were dropped, Warsh would be confirmed easily).
• Virginia passed a redistricting measure that could give the Dems as many as four additional House seats in the upcoming mid-terms ( Washington Post )
International macro

• Canada says it has no desire to significantly alter USMCA ( WSJ )
• Indonesia’s central bank left its policy rate unchanged , a move that was widely expected ( WSJ )
• Japan’s trade numbers for Mar came in a bit ahead of expectations , including exports (+11.7% vs. the Street +11%) and imports (+10.9% vs. the Street +7%) (Bloomberg)
• Russian official says Russian citizens in Transdniestria are under threat, and Moscow will take steps to protect them ( Reuters )
• South Korea’s PPI for Mar came in at +4.1% Y/Y, an acceleration from +2.5% in Feb (Bloomberg)
• UK headline CPI for Mar was inline w/the Street at +3.3% (up from +3% in Feb) and core was a bit cooler at +3.1% (vs. the Street +3.2% and down from +3.2% in Feb) while services overshot at +4.5% (vs. the Street +4.3% and up from +4.3% in Feb) ( WSJ )
Airlines/travel

• Deutsche Lufthansa said it would cut its capacity by less than 1% (eliminating ~20 K flights through Oct) in a bid to save on fuel expense .
• TUI AG cut its guidance for the FY due to fallout from the war in the Middle East (“the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the uncertainty surrounding its duration continue to limit near-term visibility and drive consumer caution”) ( TUI AG )
• UAL (United Air) reported modest upside on Q1 adjusted EPS (1.19 vs. the Street 1.09) and while the Q2 EPS guidance range falls short (they see $1-2 vs. the Street $1.75) due to the huge jump in fuel costs , the 2026 EPS mid-point of $9 is actually not far from the consensus forecast of $9.08, which is encouraging considering what’s happening with oil. The company is taking aggressive steps to address the fuel headwind , including cutting 5 points of capacity planned for the balance of the year (mgmt. says that it intends to recapture 85-100% of the fuel price increase by Q4 ).
Consumer

• Danone shares trade well in Europe after the company posted decent sales (+2.7% vs. the Street +2.65%) despite headwinds from the Middle East war and the baby formula recall, and reiterated its full-year guidance ( Danone )
• DRVN (Driven Brands) preannounced a shortfall on Q4 EBITDA ($100-110 MM vs. the Street $120 MM) and said Q1 EBITDA would fall Y/Y (the Street had been modeling ~1% growth). The restatement is taking longer than anticipated, and DRVN no longer expects to have its 2025 K ready by Apr 26 (it now says the K will arrive before June 15 ). Mgmt. tries to provide reassuring words on the balance sheet and cash flow (“continues to generate solid free cash flow. In addition, the Company’s revolving credit facility and securitization variable funding notes are currently undrawn. Management continues to believe it has adequate liquidity and operating cash flow for its needs going forward ”).
• IPAR (Interparfums) reported Q2 revenue of $345 MM, a bit below the Street forecast of $354 MM, w/the Middle East war a ~1% headwind to the top line. “ Growth continues to be more measured compared to recent years amid ongoing macroeconomic pressures and geopolitical uncertainty … we have reduced our forecast for the Middle East region due to the war ”.
• Karex , the world’s largest condom maker , said it would be forced to hike prices by up to 30% due to input cost increases stemming from the Middle East war ( FT )
• MCRI (Monarch) reported solid upside on Q1 EBITDA ($49 MM vs. the Street $44 MM), w/the beat driven by higher sales and healthy margin expansion (EBITDA margins rose ~300bp Y/Y).
• Moncler reported solid upside on Q1 revenue (+12% vs. the Street +7%), w/the core Moncler brand benefiting from strength in Asia (sales +22%) and the Americas (+7%) while EMEA underperformed (-1%).
• Reckitt Benckiser shares tumble in European trading after the company reported a shortfall on Q1 sales (+0.6% vs. the Street +1.5%) due to a mild cold and flu season, ongoing challenges in Europe, and geopolitical disruptions (the company left its full-year guidance unchanged) ( Reckitt )
• Tailored Brands, the parent company of Jos A Bank, Men’s Wearhouse, and other brands, has filed confidentially for an IPO ( Bloomberg )
• UK set to ban tobacco and vape products for anyone born after 2008 as the country looks to raise a “smoke-free generation” ( NYT )
Financials

• CB (Chubb) reported decent upside on Q1 EPS (6.82 vs. the Street 6.60), w/the beat driven by a slightly favorable combined ratio , but the big standout of the report is the robust net premium growth performance ( P&C net premiums written were +7.2%, a very healthy performance and a relief to investors given how much concern there has been about cooling top line momentum). That said, despite the 7.2% NPW growth figure, mgmt. acknowledges weakening industry conditions in certain business areas (“ both property and financial lines insurance market conditions are soft or softening , with portions of the property market softening at a rapid pace . Given inadequate price levels, we moved during the quarter to reduce exposures in our Major Accounts and E&S divisions by non-renewing a substantial percentage of our shared and layered property business that was up for renewal while purchasing additional reinsurance”).
• COF (Capital One) reported a shortfall on Q1 adjusted EPS at $4.42 (vs. the Street $4.56), with the miss driven by slightly weaker revenue (the NIM/NII came in below plan) and slightly higher provisions ($4.068 B vs. the Street $3.8 B), while they controlled expenses very well (non-interest expense dropped 9%, with marketing down 23% and operating expenses lower by 6%).
• IBKR (Interactive Brokers) reported EPS/revenue pretty much right inline w/the Street at 60c/$1.68 B although NII fell short at $904 MM (vs. the Street $948 MM).
• PRU (Prudential) announced a decision to voluntarily extend the suspension of new sales activity at Prudential of Japan by an additional 180 days (anticipation of this decision weighed on PRU shares during the Tues session).
• Wall Street banks are accelerating job cuts thanks in part to AI adoption ( NYT )
• WRB (WR Berkley) reported upside on Q1 EPS (1.30 vs. the Street 1.13), w/the beat driven by a slightly better combined ratio and higher investment income , but NPW fell short at $3.17 B (vs. the Street $3.2 B).
Healthcare

• GLP1 drugs and Medicare – Medicare will keep a pilot program running through the end of 2027 after private insurers balked at providing coverage for the drugs ( WSJ )
• ISRG (Intuitive Surgical) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS (2.50 vs. the Street 2.10) and sales ($2.77 B vs. the Street $2.61 B), and the full-year guide is tweaked up slightly (they now see sales +13.5-15.5% vs. the prior +13-15% w/ GMs of 67.5-68.5% vs. the prior 67-68% and opex up 11-14% vs. the prior +11-15%). Procedure growth in the Q at 17% overall was solid , and system shipments were ahead of plan too (although Ion shipment growth of ~6% was a bit on the soft side).
• TFX (Teleflex) – CVC and GTCR are exploring a bid for Teleflex ( Bloomberg )
Industrials/Materials/Energy

• ABB shares rally in Europe after the company reported solid upside in Q1 on sales (+11% vs. the Street +9.3%) and esp. bookings (+24% vs. the Street +9.3%), and boosted its full-year guidance (“Market momentum remains strongest in the data center segment, but also positive for grid investments”) ( ABB )
• Akzo Nobel shares rally in Europe after the company reported solid upside in Q1 on both sales and earnings/margins as the company benefited from price increases and cost controls (“While the conflict in the Middle East is impacting our cost of supply, we’re maintaining our guidance for the year . Our already announced price increases are expected to fully compensate anticipated cost impacts based on current assumptions”) ( Akzo Nobel )
• BA (Boeing) – the FAA said the new 737 MAX 7 and 10 models should receive certification by the end of the year ( Bloomberg )
• BHP now sees copper production at the upper-end of its prior guidance while the iron ore production forecast was reiterated, and the company signaled it was nearing a supply deal agreement w/China ( BHP )
• K+S issued an upside preannouncement on Q1 EBITDA and free cash flow and boosted its guidance for the year.
• Randstad shares spike in Europe after the company reported upside on Q1 organic revenue growth (+0.4% vs. the Street -0.3%) (“While geopolitical instability and limited visibility require us to remain vigilant, the trajectory in Q1 with 0.4% growth gives us confidence for the months ahead”) ( Randstad )
• SON (Sonoco) reported EPS inline w/the Street at 1.20 (vs. the Street 1.19), but EBITDA fell short ($277 MM vs. the Street $287 MM), and mgmt. now sees EPS at the low-end of the prior $5.80-6.20 guidance range due to “projected inflationary pressures and lower demand due to the uncertain macroeconomic and geopolitical environment ”.
• US says allies need to pay more for critical materials in order to break China’s stranglehold on the market ( FT )
• WFRD (Weatherford) reported a large beat on EPS ($1.49 vs. the Street $1.03), but revenue/EBITDA weren’t that far ahead of plan ($1.15 B/$233 MM vs. the Street $1.145 B/$228.5 MM), and mgmt. warned that Q2 will be softer than anticipated because of Middle East disruptions (“we expect the operational disruptions in the Middle East to cloud near term visibility as the geopolitical backdrop remains volatile and therefore creates uncertainty. Given the likelihood that it will take at minimum several weeks for activity levels to normalize, logistics to stabilize and incremental costs to come down, our second quarter results are expected to be softer than previously anticipated , with performance within the range dependent on the timing of these factors. At the same time, assuming that the conflict is fully behind us by the end of the quarter, we have increased confidence in the second-half ramp that positions us for a stronger 2027”).
M&A/Strategic Actions

• Arsenal Resources is exploring a sale and could fetch as much as $1.5 B ( Bloomberg )
• Space X announced after the close plans to buy Cursor later this year for $60 B (or pay Cursor a $10 B fee for the work being done together) in a deal that would bring together x AI and Cursor to create “the world’s best coding and knowledge work AI” . What does this mean? While AI chatbots like Chat GPT get a lot of attention, the real area of focus for the industry in terms of monetizing all the capex are coding tools (which are used not only for quickly writing software, but also creating, deploying, and managing agents), with Anthropic’s Claude Code the highest-profile product followed by Open AI’s Codex and Cursor. Musk is making a big pitch on space-based data centers , but the AI part of the Space X story is dubious at best , with x AI trailing behind the industry’s leaders in terms of market and mindshare, but the addition of Cursor makes the investment thesis much more compelling , which should help the upcoming IPO. $60 B is a lot of money, but if Cursor helps Space X achieve the upper end of its valuation goals (i.e. $2 T), it would be money well spent.
• Tailored Brands, the parent company of Jos A Bank, Men’s Wearhouse, and other brands, has filed confidentially for an IPO ( Bloomberg )
• TFX (Teleflex) – CVC and GTCR are exploring a bid for Teleflex ( Bloomberg )
• TMUS (T-Mobile)/Deutsche Telecom – Deutsche Telecom is considering a full merger w/its US subsidiary, TMUS ( Bloomberg )
TMT

• ADBE (Adobe) announced a $25 B buyback authorization , which is 25% of the market cap at the present time (“Our new $25 billion share repurchase authorization is a direct expression of confidence in our robust cash flow and the long-term value we are delivering to investors ”). The $25 B is a huge number, but a lot of legacy software companies have big buybacks in place, and it hasn’t been enough to erase the existential risks/concerns created by AI .
• Anthropic is looking into whether unauthorized users were able to access its Claude Mythos model ( FT )
• ASM Int’l shares spike in European trading after the company reported strong upside in Q1 on sales and margins (“AI-led demand accelerated further in the quarter, underpinned by strong customer investments in capacity to drive long-term AI infrastructure expansion”), and provided a Q2 sales outlook is ahead of expectations too (they see sales of EU980 MM vs. the Street EU887 MM).
• CALX (Calix) reported modest upside on Q1 EPS/revenue (40c/$280 MM vs. the Street 38c/$277.5 MM) and added $100 MM to the buyback , but the Q2 guidance is mixed , w/ better revenue ($290 MM vs. the Street $281 MM) but slightly weaker EPS (40c vs. the Street 42c) as memory prices weigh on margins (the GM guide for Q2 of 55.8% is nearly 200bp below the Street because of memory ).
• HCL Tech shares tumbled in India after the company reported underwhelming results and provided disappointing guidance ( Reuters )
• MANH (Manhattan Associates) reported decent upside on Q1 EPS/revenue (1.24/$282 MM vs. the Street 1.12/$274 MM), and RPOs were better too , and the full-year guidance is increased (they now see EPS up 5-6% vs. the prior 0-3% w/sales up 6-7% vs. the prior +5-7%). “Manhattan is off to a strong start to 2026. On solid and broad-based demand, we accelerated our Q1 revenue growth and delivered better than expected bookings. While macro volatility persists, Manhattan’s fundamentals are solid”.
• META (Meta) is installing tracking software on employee computers to help train its AI models ( Reuters )
• Open AI faces a criminal investigation in Florida over the alleged role played by Chat GPT in a mass shooting last year ( WSJ )
• SK Hynix said it would invest ~$13 B in a new South Korean memory plant ( Reuters )

market_in_a_minute_macro:
Market in a Minute (macro) - The US and Iran have actually made significant progress behind the scenes on key issues (including Iran’s nuclear program) and could strike a deal fairly quickly if/when the two sides agree to meet ( WSJ ); Trump’s ceasefire suspension is “not going to be open-ended” and Iran only has “another 3-5 days” to engage in negotiations (White House officials still believe a deal is possible but are encountering difficulties in negotiating given divisions within the Tehran gov’t ) ( Axios ); A White House official said there is still a chance that US and Iran hold face-to-face meetings soon , and a trip could be arranged quickly if Tehran gives indications it is prepared to negotiate ( CNN ); Iran’s military still maintains significant capabilities despite the recent US/Israeli bombing campaign ( NBC News ); US has significantly depleted its stockpiles of critical missiles during the war with Iran ( CNN ); Wall Street banks are accelerating job cuts thanks in part to AI adoption ( NYT ).
•

However, intense distrust between Washington and Tehran , along with the ongoing blockade , are preventing talks from taking place , and raising the risks of unintended escalation (Iran fired on two container ships overnight). Away from geopolitics, the big area of focus is on earnings . In aggregate, results for the Q1 season have been coming in strong , with companies performing well despite the Iran/Hormuz-linked geopolitical pressures . However, travel-linked firms are experiencing a massive cost burden given what’s happening w/oil ( United Air and TUI AG both cut their full-year outlooks because of oil). Some of the big upside standouts this morning on the earnings front include ABB, Akzo Nobel, ASM Int’l, ASMPT, CB, Danone, K+S, MANH , and Randstad . Some of the big downside standouts this morning on the earnings front include CALX, COF , HCL Tech , and Reckitt Benckiser .

Danone shares trade well in Europe after the company posted decent sales (+2.7% vs. the Street +2.65%) despite headwinds from the Middle East war and the baby formula recall, and reiterated its full-year guidance ( Danone ); HCL Tech shares tumbled in India after the company reported underwhelming results and provided disappointing guidance ( Reuters ); K+S issued an upside preannouncement on Q1 EBITDA and free cash flow and boosted its guidance for the year; Karex , the world’s largest condom maker, said it would be forced to hike prices by up to 30% due to input cost increases stemming from the Middle East war ( FT ); Randstad shares spike in Europe after the company reported upside on Q1 organic revenue growth (+0.4% vs. the Street -0.3%) (“While geopolitical instability and limited visibility require us to remain vigilant, the trajectory in Q1 with 0.4% growth gives us confidence for the months ahead”) ( Randstad ); TUI AG cut its guidance for the FY due to fallout from the war in the Middle East (“the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the uncertainty surrounding its duration continue to limit near-term visibility and drive consumer caution”) ( TUI AG )

Iran

• Trump issued a social media post at ~4:10pm ET in which he said the ceasefire deadline has been postponed indefinitely (it was expected to expire Wed night). After insisting in recent days that the deadline would not be moved, Trump stayed true to his word – he eliminated it instead (please note the sarcasm in the preceding sentence). As has been clear for weeks, Trump is stuck – he has no good options to kinetically escalate (which is why he continues not to do so), he can’t simply exit , and the status quo (with Hormuz shut) isn’t tenable , and as a result, his only realistic path forward is pushing for some type of a “deal” , and it’s likely one arrives in the relatively near term (we put “deal” in quotes as whatever gets announced won’t be much different than a grudging, nonbinding détente between the two sides with some deliverables but nothing that solves the core problems in perpetuity ). While there is still a bit of skepticism and cynicism in the market about Iran , most are of the view that Operation Epic Fury is past its peak , with an agreement of some sort more likely than not .
• Trump’s ceasefire suspension is “not going to be open-ended” and Iran only has “another 3-5 days” to engage in negotiations (White House officials still believe a deal is possible but are encountering difficulties in negotiating given divisions within the Tehran gov’t ) ( Axios )
• The US and Iran have actually made significant progress behind the scenes on key issues (including Iran’s nuclear program) and could strike a deal fairly quickly if/when the two sides agree to meet ( WSJ )
• A White House official said there is still a chance that US and Iran hold face-to-face meetings soon , and a trip could be arranged quickly if Tehran gives indications it is prepared to negotiate ( CNN )
• Iran’s military still maintains significant capabilities despite the recent US/Israeli bombing campaign ( NBC News )
• US has significantly depleted its stockpiles of critical missiles during the war with Iran ( CNN )
• Global oil market will lose ~1 B barrels of crude and refined products due to the war in Iran, even if the conflict ended tomorrow ( FT )
• Global food market at risk of a significant shock due to the ongoing closure of Hormuz ( FT )
• At least 34 tankers with links to Iran have managed to travel in/out of Hormuz since the US blockade commenced ( FT )
us_macro_section:
US macro/Washington

• We didn’t think Warsh’s testimony on Tuesday was particularly notable , with most of his answers either anodyne or vague (he wants rates lower and the balance sheet smaller but didn’t provide much specificity around either policy objective). The real issue w/Warsh is that he probably won’t be confirmed by the time Powell’s term ends on May 15 , which could prompt Trump to (attempt to) fire Powell as chair (if the DOJ investigation into the Fed were dropped, Warsh would be confirmed easily).
• Virginia passed a redistricting measure that could give the Dems as many as four additional House seats in the upcoming mid-terms ( Washington Post )
international_macro_section:
International macro

• Canada says it has no desire to significantly alter USMCA ( WSJ )
• Indonesia’s central bank left its policy rate unchanged , a move that was widely expected ( WSJ )
• Japan’s trade numbers for Mar came in a bit ahead of expectations , including exports (+11.7% vs. the Street +11%) and imports (+10.9% vs. the Street +7%) (Bloomberg)
• Russian official says Russian citizens in Transdniestria are under threat, and Moscow will take steps to protect them ( Reuters )
• South Korea’s PPI for Mar came in at +4.1% Y/Y, an acceleration from +2.5% in Feb (Bloomberg)
• UK headline CPI for Mar was inline w/the Street at +3.3% (up from +3% in Feb) and core was a bit cooler at +3.1% (vs. the Street +3.2% and down from +3.2% in Feb) while services overshot at +4.5% (vs. the Street +4.3% and up from +4.3% in Feb) ( WSJ )
Airlines/travel

• Deutsche Lufthansa said it would cut its capacity by less than 1% (eliminating ~20 K flights through Oct) in a bid to save on fuel expense .
• TUI AG cut its guidance for the FY due to fallout from the war in the Middle East (“the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the uncertainty surrounding its duration continue to limit near-term visibility and drive
energy_section:
energy, real estate, tech, chemicals, and basic resources outperform . Akzo Nobel, ASM Int’l, Danone, K+S, and Randstad are all rallying on earnings. Reckitt Benckiser is getting hit on back of its report. Stocks in Asia saw mixed price action on Wednesday: TPX -0.67%, NKY +0.4%, Hang Seng -1.22%, HSCEI -1.59%, SHCOMP +0.52%, Shenzhen +1%, Taiwan TAIEX +0.73%, Korea KOSPI +0.46%, Australia ASX 200 -1.18%, and India is down 70-80bp. Treasuries are pretty flat across the curve, and Fed expectations are holding steady. The market is pricing in ~9bp of FOMC rate cuts this year. Brent is climbing 110bp despite the indefinite ceasefire extension. Gold is advancing 90bp while silver rises 200bp. Bitcoin is spiking 310bp (to ~$78 K).

Airlines/travel

• Deutsche Lufthansa said it would cut its capacity by less than 1% (eliminating ~20 K flights through Oct) in a bid to save on fuel expense .
• TUI AG cut its guidance for the FY due to fallout from the war in the Middle East (“the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the uncertainty surrounding its duration continue to limit near-term visibility and drive consumer caution”) ( TUI AG )
• UAL (United Air) reported modest upside on Q1 adjusted EPS (1.19 vs. the Street 1.09) and while the Q2 EPS guidance range falls short (they see $1-2 vs. the Street $1.75) due to the huge jump in fuel costs , the 2026 EPS mid-point of $9 is actually not far from the consensus forecast of $9.08, which is encouraging considering what’s happening with oil. The company is taking aggressive steps to address the fuel headwind , including cutting 5 points of capacity planned for the balance of the year (mgmt. says that it intends to recapture 85-100% of the fuel price increase by Q4 ).
Consumer

• Danone shares trade well in Europe after the company posted decent sales (+2.7% vs. the Street +2.65%) despite headwinds from the Middle East war and the baby formula recall, and reiterated its full-year guidance ( Danone )
• DRVN (Driven Brands) preannounced a shortfall on Q4 EBITDA ($100-110 MM vs. the Street $120 MM) and said Q1 EBITDA would fall Y/Y (the Street had been modeling ~1% growth). The restatement is taking longer than anticipated, and DRVN no longer expects to have its 2025 K ready by Apr 26 (it now says the K will arrive before June 15 ). Mgmt. tries to provide reassuring words on the balance sheet and cash flow (“continues to generate solid free cash flow. In addition, the Company’s revolving credit facility and securitization variable funding notes are currently undrawn. Management continues to believe it has adequate liquidity and operating cash flow for its needs going forward ”).
• IPAR (Interparfums) reported Q2 revenue of $345 MM, a bit below the Street forecast of $354 MM, w/the Middle East war a ~1% headwind to the top line. “ Growth continues to be more measured compared to recent years amid ongoing macroeconomic pressures and geopolitical uncertainty … we have reduced our forecast for the Middle East region due to the war ”.
• Karex , the world’s largest condom maker , said it would be forced to hike prices by up to 30% due to input cost increases stemming from the Middle East war ( FT )
• MCRI (Monarch) reported solid upside on Q1 EBITDA ($49 MM vs. the Street $44 MM), w/the beat driven by higher sales and healthy margin expansion (EBITDA margins rose ~300bp Y/Y).
• Moncler reported solid upside on Q1 revenue (+12% vs. the Street +7%), w/the core Moncler brand benefiting from strength in Asia (sales +22%) and the Americas (+7%) while EMEA underperformed (-1%).
• Reckitt Benckiser shares tumble in European trading after the company reported a shortfall on Q1 sales (+0.6% vs. the Street +1.5%) due to a mild cold and flu season, ongoing challenges in Europe, and geopolitical disruptions (the company left its full-year guidance unchanged) ( Reckitt )
• Tailored Brands, the parent company of Jos A Bank, Men’s Wearhouse, and other brands, has filed confidentially for an IPO ( Bloomberg )
• UK set to ban tobacco and vape products for anyone born after 2008 as the country looks to raise a “smoke-free generation” ( NYT )
tmt_section:
TMT

• ADBE (Adobe) announced a $25 B buyback authorization , which is 25% of the market cap at the present time (“Our new $25 billion share repurchase authorization is a direct expression of confidence in our robust cash flow and the long-term value we are delivering to investors ”). The $25 B is a huge number, but a lot of legacy software companies have big buybacks in place, and it hasn’t been enough to erase the existential risks/concerns created by AI .
• Anthropic is looking into whether unauthorized users were able to access its Claude Mythos model ( FT )
• ASM Int’l shares spike in European trading after the company reported strong upside in Q1 on sales and margins (“AI-led demand accelerated further in the quarter, underpinned by strong customer investments in capacity to drive long-term AI infrastructure expansion”), and provided a Q2 sales outlook is ahead of expectations too (they see sales of EU980 MM vs. the Street EU887 MM).
• CALX (Calix) reported modest upside on Q1 EPS/revenue (40c/$280 MM vs. the Street 38c/$277.5 MM) and added $100 MM to the buyback , but the Q2 guidance is mixed , w/ better revenue ($290 MM vs. the Street $281 MM) but slightly weaker EPS (40c vs. the Street 42c) as memory prices weigh on margins (the GM guide for Q2 of 55.8% is nearly 200bp below the Street because of memory ).
• HCL Tech shares tumbled in India after the company reported underwhelming results and provided disappointing guidance ( Reuters )
• MANH (Manhattan Associates) reported decent upside on Q1 EPS/revenue (1.24/$282 MM vs. the Street 1.12/$274 MM), and RPOs were better too , and the full-year guidance is increased (they now see EPS up 5-6% vs. the prior 0-3% w/sales up 6-7% vs. the prior +5-7%). “Manhattan is off to a strong start to 2026. On solid and broad-based demand, we accelerated our Q1 revenue growth and delivered better than expected bookings. While macro volatility persists, Manhattan’s fundamentals are solid”.
• META (Meta) is installing tracking software on employee computers to help train its AI models ( Reuters )
• Open AI faces a criminal investigation in Florida over the alleged role played by Chat GPT in a mass shooting last year ( WSJ )
• SK Hynix said it would invest ~$13 B in a new South Korean memory plant ( Reuters )
• Space X announced after the close plans to buy Cursor later this year for $60 B (or pay Cursor a $10 B fee for the work being done together) in a deal that would bring together x AI and Cursor to create “the world’s best coding and knowledge work AI” . What does this mean? While AI chatbots like Chat GPT get a lot of attention, the real area of focus for the industry in terms of monetizing all the capex are coding tools (which are used not only for quickly writing software, but also creating, deploying, and managing agents), with Anthropic’s Claude Code the highest-profile product followed by Open AI’s Codex and Cursor. Musk is making a big pitch on space-based data centers , but the AI part of the Space X story is dubious at best , with x AI trailing behind the industry’s leaders in terms of market and mindshare, but the addition of Cursor makes the investment thesis much more compelling , which should help the upcoming IPO. $60 B is a lot of money, but if Cursor helps Space X achieve the upper end of its valuation goals (i.e. $2 T), it would be money well spent.
• Space X will warn in its IPO documents that plans for space-based data centers may not be commercially viable ( Reuters )
• TMUS (T-Mobile)/Deutsche Telecom – Deutsche Telecom is considering a full merger w/its US subsidiary, TMUS ( Bloomberg )
